evidence_quotes.quote: the growing trend of game discontinuation and server shutdowns that render purchased products completely unusable.
When the servers were shut down, the game stopped working completely, even in solo mode.
The Crew: A racing game I purchased that was fully dependent on online servers.
Battleborn: I purchased the game and also spent additional money on microtransactions.
When the game was discontinued, I lost access entirely and received no refunds.
There are no refunds, no offline fallback options, and no guarantees.
Requirements for publishers to maintain reasonable access to purchased content, especially in cases where no alternative is provided.
Clear communication and advance notice before shutdowns, along with options such as offline patches or community-hosted server support where feasible.
Transparency about product lifespans at the time of sale.

digital goods are not disposable and should not be treated as temporary by default.

paragraphs.text: Dear European Commission, I am writing to express my support for strong and enforceable digital consumer protections under the proposed Digital Fairness Act, particularly in relation to online and connected video games. As a dedicated player and paying customer, I have been directly affected by the growing trend of game discontinuation and server shutdowns that render purchased products completely unusable. Modern games increasingly rely on online infrastructure, even for single-player or non-competitive modes. While this enables new forms of entertainment, it also exposes consumers to significant risks when publishers decide to retire a title. I believe consumers deserve protection when companies remove access to digital goods they have paid for. In my personal experience, I have had several games become entirely inaccessible despite having invested time, money, and trust in them: The Crew: A racing game I purchased that was fully dependent on online servers. When the servers were shut down, the game stopped working completely, even in solo mode. This was not just disappointingit felt like I lost something I owned and enjoyed, without recourse. Anthem: A title I currently play, which is also dependent on online infrastructure. I know that support and servers for Anthem will be discontinued (January 12, 2026), and I will again lose access to a game I paid for and enjoyed. Battleborn: I purchased the game and also spent additional money on microtransactions. When the game was discontinued, I lost access entirely and received no refunds. These experiences are frustrating and erode consumer confidence in digital markets. There are no refunds, no offline fallback options, and no guarantees. As digital content becomes the norm, the law must evolve to reflect that digital goods are not disposable and should not be treated as temporary by default. Therefore, I respectfully urge the European Commission to ensure the Digital Fairness Act includes: Requirements for publishers to maintain reasonable access to purchased content, especially in cases where no alternative is provided. Clear communication and advance notice before shutdowns, along with options such as offline patches or community-hosted server support where feasible. Transparency about product lifespans at the time of sale. The right to a refund or compensation when paid-for products are no longer usable due to company decisions. Digital fairness must include the right to retain access to what weve paid for. I thank you for your attention to this matter and hope the final legislation reflects the needs of todays digital consumers. Sincerely, Márcio
